steps.title: Identify the appositive type
steps.content: The phrase "thymol and methane" follows "compounds" and identifies which specific compounds are meant. Because those two names are the only way to distinguish the subject — they are essential to understanding what the sentence refers to — the appositive is restrictive. Restrictive appositives take no commas; non-restrictive (parenthetical) appositives require commas on both sides.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — compounds, thymol and methane: opens with a comma but provides no closing comma — this is unmatched punctuation that incorrectly implies the names are parenthetical while leaving the appositive unclosed. ✗
  • Choice B — compounds, thymol and methane,: commas on both sides treat the names as non-restrictive and parenthetical, but the names are essential — they identify which compounds. ✗
  • Choice C — compounds thymol and methane: no commas, correctly treating "thymol and methane" as a restrictive appositive that specifies which gaseous compounds are meant. ✓
  • Choice D — compounds thymol and methane,: a trailing comma with no opening comma is unmatched and creates an awkward break between the subject and its verb "have." ✗
That matches Choice C — compounds thymol and methane.
